// handbook
Astro
v5 · All Concepts · From Zero
Getting Started
01 What is Astro? 02 Installation 03 Project Structure
Core
04 .astro Components 05 Pages & Routing 06 Layouts 07 Islands Architecture
Data
08 Props & Slots 09 Data Fetching 10 Content Collections
Advanced
11 Styling 12 Assets & Images 13 Integrations 14 SSR & Adapters 15 Middleware 16 Actions 17 View Transitions 18 Env Variables 19 Deployment
01 · Introduction

The Astro
Handbook

Everything you need to go from zero to ship with Astro — the web framework built for content-heavy, fast websites. No fluff. Just concepts, code, and clarity.

Islands Architecture Zero JS by Default Content Collections SSG + SSR Framework Agnostic React / Vue / Svelte Tailwind Ready

What is Astro?

Astro is a web framework focused on content-driven websites — blogs, documentation, marketing pages, e-commerce. Its core philosophy: ship less JavaScript to the browser. HTML is rendered at build time, and interactive components (called Islands) are hydrated on-demand.

⚡ Zero JS by Default

Every page ships as static HTML. JS only loads when you opt in via client directives.

🏝️ Islands

Interactive UI components exist as isolated islands in an otherwise static sea.

🔌 Bring Your UI

Use React, Vue, Svelte, Solid, Preact — or just plain HTML. All in one project.

Astro vs Other Frameworks

Framework Default Output Best For Hydration
Astro Static HTML Content sites Partial (Islands)
Next.js SSR/SSG hybrid React apps Full page
Nuxt SSR/SSG hybrid Vue apps Full page
SvelteKit SSR + adapter Svelte apps Full page
Remix SSR first Web apps Full page
02 · Installation

Getting Started

Astro requires Node.js v18.17.1+. The fastest way to bootstrap a project is the official CLI wizard.

Run the create wizard

# npm
npm create astro@latest

# pnpm
pnpm create astro@latest

# yarn
yarn create astro

Answer the prompts

The wizard asks for: project name, template (empty / blog / portfolio), TypeScript preference, and package manager. Houston (Astro's mascot) guides you through.

Start the dev server

cd my-project
npm run dev
# → http://localhost:4321

Key CLI Commands

Command Action
npm run dev Start dev server with HMR
npm run build Build production output → dist/
npm run preview Preview production build locally
npx astro add react Auto-install integration
npx astro check Type-check project
npx astro info Debug environment info
03 · Project Structure

Project Structure

Astro enforces a specific directory convention. Understanding it upfront saves confusion later.

🔑 Key Rule
Only files inside src/pages/ become routable URLs. Everything else in src/ is imported but never exposed directly.

astro.config.mjs

This is the heart of your Astro project. All integrations, output mode, and base path live here.

astro.config.mjs
import { defineConfig } from 'astro/config';
import react from '@astrojs/react';
import tailwind from '@astrojs/tailwind';

export default defineConfig({
  site: 'https://mysite.com',   // needed for sitemaps, canonical URLs
  base: '/',
  output: 'static',             // 'static' | 'server' | 'hybrid'
  integrations: [react(), tailwind()],
  vite: {
    // pass-through Vite config here
  }
});
04 · Components

.astro Components

The .astro file is Astro's own component format. It borrows JSX-like syntax but compiles to pure HTML — no runtime framework required.

Anatomy of a .astro File

Every .astro file is split into two parts by a triple-dash fence (---):

src/components/Card.astro
---
// 1. COMPONENT SCRIPT (runs only at build/request time — server-side)
// This is where you import, fetch, define props, run logic

const { title, description } = Astro.props;
const date = new Date().toISOString();
---

<!-- 2. COMPONENT TEMPLATE (JSX-like HTML) -->
<div class="card">
  <h2>{title}</h2>
  <p>{description}</p>
  <small>{date}</small>
</div>

<!-- 3. OPTIONAL: scoped styles -->
<style>
  .card { border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 1rem; }
</style>
⚡ Important
The component script runs at build time (or per-request in SSR). It's never in the browser. You can use await, call databases, read the file system — anything Node.js can do.

Expressions in Templates

---
const items = ['Apple', 'Banana', 'Cherry'];
const isVisible = true;
---

<!-- Rendering a list -->
<ul>
  {items.map((item) => (
    <li>{item}</li>
  ))}
</ul>

<!-- Conditional rendering -->
{isVisible && <p>I am visible</p>}
{isVisible ? <p>Yes</p> : <p>No</p>}

<!-- Set raw HTML (use carefully!) -->
<div set:html={rawHtmlString} />

Importing & Using Components

---
import Card from '../components/Card.astro';
import Button from '../components/Button.astro';
---

<Card title="Hello" description="World" />
<Button variant="primary">Click me</Button>
05 · Pages & Routing

Pages & Routing

Astro uses file-based routing. Any file in src/pages/ with a supported extension becomes a URL.

Supported Page Formats

File URL Notes
src/pages/index.astro / Home page
src/pages/about.astro /about
src/pages/blog/index.astro /blog Folder index
src/pages/blog/[slug].astro /blog/anything Dynamic route
src/pages/[...all].astro Catch-all Rest params
src/pages/rss.xml.js /rss.xml API / feed endpoint

Dynamic Routes — getStaticPaths()

For dynamic routes in static mode, you must export getStaticPaths() to tell Astro which URLs to generate.

src/pages/blog/[slug].astro
---
export async function getStaticPaths() {
  // return every possible [slug] value
  return [
    { params: { slug: 'first-post' }, props: { title: 'First Post' } },
    { params: { slug: 'second-post' }, props: { title: 'Second Post' } },
  ];
}

const { slug } = Astro.params;
const { title } = Astro.props;
---

<h1>{title}</h1>
<p>Slug: {slug}</p>

API Endpoints

Files ending in .js or .ts in pages/ become API endpoints (JSON, XML, RSS feeds).

src/pages/api/hello.json.ts
import type { APIRoute } from 'astro';

export const GET: APIRoute = ({ params, request }) => {
  return new Response(
    JSON.stringify({ message: 'Hello, World!' }),
    { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' } }
  );
};
💡 Tip
You can also export POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH from the same endpoint file.
06 · Layouts

Layouts

Layouts are wrapper components that provide shared structure (header, footer, `<head>`) around page content via <slot />.

src/layouts/BaseLayout.astro
---
interface Props {
  title: string;
  description?: string;
}

const { title, description = 'My website' } = Astro.props;
---

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width" />
  <title>{title}</title>
  <meta name="description" content={description} />
</head>
<body>
  <header><nav>...</nav></header>

  <main>
    <slot />   <!-- page content renders here -->
  </main>

  <footer>...</footer>
</body>
</html>

Using a Layout in a Page

src/pages/about.astro
---
import BaseLayout from '../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
---

<BaseLayout title="About Me" description="Who I am">
  <h1>About Me</h1>
  <p>I build stuff on the web.</p>
</BaseLayout>

Named Slots

Need multiple insertion points? Use named slots.

<!-- In layout: -->
<slot name="header" />   <!-- named slot -->
<slot />                 <!-- default slot -->
<slot name="footer" />   <!-- named slot -->

<!-- In page using the layout: -->
<MyLayout>
  <h1 slot="header">Page Title</h1>   <!-- goes to header slot -->
  <p>Body content</p>                 <!-- goes to default slot -->
</MyLayout>

Markdown Layouts

Markdown/MDX files can declare their layout in frontmatter.

src/content/blog/my-post.md
---
layout: ../../layouts/BlogPost.astro
title: My First Post
pubDate: 2024-01-01
---

# Hello World
This is markdown content...
07 · Islands Architecture

Islands Architecture

This is Astro's most important concept. Most of your page ships as zero-JavaScript HTML. Only the parts that need interactivity — Islands — load their framework and hydrate in the browser.

Client Directives — How to Hydrate

Add a client:* directive to any component to make it an Island. Without it, a React/Vue component renders as static HTML only.

Directive When JS Loads Use Case
client:load Immediately on page load Visible, critical UI
client:idle When browser is idle Less critical, below fold
client:visible When component enters viewport Lazy-loaded, far down page
client:media="(query)" When media query matches Mobile-only nav, etc.
client:only="react"` Browser only (no SSR) Components using browser APIs 
---
import Search from './Search.jsx';      // React component
import Counter from './Counter.svelte';  // Svelte component
import Chart from './Chart.vue';        // Vue component
---

<!-- No directive = static HTML, no JS -->
<Search />

<!-- Hydrate immediately -->
<Search client:load />

<!-- Wait for idle -->
<Counter client:idle />

<!-- Only when in viewport -->
<Chart client:visible />

<!-- Mobile only -->
<MobileNav client:media="(max-width: 768px)" />
⚠️ Islands are Isolated
Islands don't share state with each other by default. If you need shared state across islands, use a framework-agnostic store like nanostores (officially recommended by Astro).
08 · Props & Slots

Props & Slots

Defining & Typing Props

---
interface Props {
  name: string;
  age?: number;          // optional
  variant: 'primary' | 'secondary';
}

const { name, age = 0, variant = 'primary' } = Astro.props;
---

<div class={`btn btn-${variant}`}>
  {name} - {age}
</div>

Astro.props vs Destructuring

// Both are equivalent:
const { title } = Astro.props;       // destructured
const title = Astro.props.title;   // direct access

Passing All HTML Attributes

---
const { class: className, ...rest } = Astro.props;
---
<button {...rest} class={`base-btn ${className}`}>
  <slot />
</button>

Slot Fallback Content

<slot>
  <!-- This renders if no slot content is provided -->
  <p>Default content here</p>
</slot>
09 · Data Fetching

Data Fetching

Since the component script runs on the server (or at build time), you can use await freely — no useEffect, no loading states needed for initial data.

Fetching External APIs

src/pages/posts.astro
---
// Top-level await works inside .astro files!
const response = await fetch('https://api.example.com/posts');
const posts = await response.json();
---

<ul>
  {posts.map((post) => (
    <li><a href={post.url}>{post.title}</a></li>
  ))}
</ul>

Reading Local Files

---
import fs from 'node:fs/promises';

const data = await fs.readFile('./data.json', 'utf-8');
const json = JSON.parse(data);
---

Astro.glob() — Import Multiple Files

Astro.glob() lets you import multiple files at once with a glob pattern — great for markdown posts, JSON files, etc.

---
// Import all markdown files from a folder
const posts = await Astro.glob('../content/blog/*.md');

// Sort by date descending
posts.sort((a, b) =>
  new Date(b.frontmatter.pubDate) - new Date(a.frontmatter.pubDate)
);
---

{posts.map((post) => (
  <a href={post.url}>{post.frontmatter.title}</a>
))}
ℹ️ Modern Alternative
Astro.glob() still works but Content Collections (next section) are the preferred, type-safe approach for managing content in Astro v2+.
10 · Content Collections

Content Collections v2+

Content Collections give you a type-safe, schema-validated way to manage local content — markdown, MDX, YAML, JSON. Think of it as a local database for your content.

1. Define Your Schema

src/content/config.ts
import { z, defineCollection } from 'astro:content';

const blogCollection = defineCollection({
  type: 'content',    // 'content' for md/mdx, 'data' for json/yaml
  schema: z.object({
    title: z.string(),
    pubDate: z.date(),
    author: z.string().default('Anonymous'),
    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
    draft: z.boolean().default(false),
    cover: z.string().optional(),
  }),
});

export const collections = {
  'blog': blogCollection,
};

2. Write Your Content

src/content/blog/my-post.md
---
title: "My First Post"
pubDate: 2024-01-15
author: "ZEZO"
tags: ["astro", "webdev"]
---

# Hello from Markdown
Content here...

3. Query the Collection

src/pages/blog/index.astro
---
import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';

// getCollection returns typed, validated entries
const allPosts = await getCollection('blog');

// Filter out drafts
const published = allPosts.filter((post) => !post.data.draft);
---

{published.map((post) => (
  <article>
    <a href={`/blog/${post.slug}`}>{post.data.title}</a>
  </article>
))}

4. Render a Single Entry

src/pages/blog/[slug].astro
---
import { getCollection, getEntry } from 'astro:content';

export async function getStaticPaths() {
  const posts = await getCollection('blog');
  return posts.map((post) => ({
    params: { slug: post.slug },
    props: { post },
  }));
}

const { post } = Astro.props;
const { Content } = await post.render();  // renders markdown to component
---

<h1>{post.data.title}</h1>
<Content />   <!-- renders markdown body -->
11 · Styling

Styling

Scoped styles are the default in .astro files. They only apply to elements in that component.

<h1>Only this h1 gets styled</h1>

<style>
  /* Automatically scoped — won't leak */
  h1 {
    color: red;
    font-size: 2rem;
  }
</style>

Use is:global or a separate CSS file imported in your layout for truly global styles.

<!-- Option 1: is:global modifier -->
<style is:global>
  body { margin: 0; font-family: sans-serif; }
</style>

<!-- Option 2: Import CSS file -->
---
import '../styles/global.css';
---

Install Tailwind with one command. Astro's integration auto-configures everything.

npx astro add tailwind

# Then just use classes in your templates:
<button class="bg-teal-500 hover:bg-teal-600 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded">
  Click Me
</button>

CSS Modules work via Vite, producing locally-scoped class names.

src/components/Button.module.css
.button { background: teal; padding: 8px 16px; }
src/components/Button.astro
---
import styles from './Button.module.css';
---
<button class={styles.button}><slot /></button>

Dynamic Class Names

---
const { variant = 'default' } = Astro.props;
---

<!-- Using template literals -->
<div class={`card card-${variant}`}>...</div>

<!-- Using class:list directive (Astro built-in) -->
<div class:list={['card', { 'card-active': isActive }, variant]}>...</div>
12 · Assets & Images

Assets & Images

Astro has a built-in image optimization pipeline. Images in src/assets/ are processed; images in public/ are served as-is.

The <Image /> Component

---
import { Image } from 'astro:assets';
import hero from '../assets/hero.jpg';
---

<!-- Astro auto-optimizes: WebP, sizing, lazy loading -->
<Image
  src={hero}
  alt="Hero image"
  width={800}
  height={400}
  format="webp"
  quality={80}
/>

The <Picture /> Component

---
import { Picture } from 'astro:assets';
import photo from '../assets/photo.jpg';
---

<Picture
  src={photo}
  alt="Responsive photo"
  formats={['avif', 'webp']}
  widths={[400, 800, 1200]}
/>
src/assets/ (Processed)

Images here are imported and processed by Astro. They get optimized, converted to WebP/AVIF, and get hashed filenames for caching.

public/ (Pass-through)

Files here are copied as-is. Use for favicons, robots.txt, OG images that need a fixed, predictable URL.

13 · Integrations

Integrations

Integrations extend Astro's capabilities — UI frameworks, styling, deployment adapters, analytics, sitemaps, and more.

Adding Integrations

# Official integrations — auto-configures everything
npx astro add react       # React + React DOM
npx astro add vue         # Vue 3
npx astro add svelte      # Svelte
npx astro add solid-js    # Solid
npx astro add tailwind    # Tailwind CSS
npx astro add mdx         # MDX support
npx astro add sitemap     # Auto-generates sitemap.xml
npx astro add node        # Node.js adapter (SSR)
npx astro add vercel      # Vercel adapter (SSR)

Using Multiple UI Frameworks

---
import ReactCounter from './ReactCounter.jsx';
import VueSearch from './VueSearch.vue';
import SvelteModal from './SvelteModal.svelte';
---

<!-- All work together on one page! -->
<ReactCounter client:load />
<VueSearch client:idle />
<SvelteModal client:visible />

Official Integration Catalog

⚛️ React

@astrojs/react

💚 Vue

@astrojs/vue

🔥 Svelte

@astrojs/svelte

💧 Solid

@astrojs/solid-js

🎨 Tailwind

@astrojs/tailwind

📝 MDX

@astrojs/mdx

🗺️ Sitemap

@astrojs/sitemap

🔍 Partytown

@astrojs/partytown

📦 Node

@astrojs/node

14 · SSR & Adapters

SSR & Output Modes

By default, Astro renders everything at build time (SSG). You can switch to server-side rendering per-page or for the whole project.

Three Output Modes

Mode Config Behavior
static output: 'static' All pages pre-rendered at build time (default)
server output: 'server' All pages rendered on each request
hybrid output: 'hybrid' Pages are static by default; opt pages into SSR

Switching to SSR

astro.config.mjs
import vercel from '@astrojs/vercel/serverless';

export default defineConfig({
  output: 'server',    // or 'hybrid'
  adapter: vercel(),  // deployment target adapter
});

Hybrid Mode — Opt-in SSR per Page

src/pages/dashboard.astro
---
// In 'hybrid' mode, opt this page into SSR:
export const prerender = false;  // SSR
// export const prerender = true;   // pre-render (default in hybrid)

// In 'server' mode, opt into static:
export const prerender = true;
---

Reading Request Data in SSR

---
export const prerender = false;

const { request, url, cookies, redirect } = Astro;

// Read URL params
const id = url.searchParams.get('id');

// Read cookies
const token = cookies.get('auth_token')?.value;

// Redirect
if (!token) return redirect('/login');

// Read POST body
if (request.method === 'POST') {
  const body = await request.json();
}
---
15 · Middleware

Middleware

Middleware runs on every request before the page renders. Perfect for auth checks, setting locals, logging, A/B testing.

src/middleware.ts
import { defineMiddleware } from 'astro:middleware';

export const onRequest = defineMiddleware(
  async ({ request, locals, cookies, redirect }, next) => {

  // Set data on locals — accessible in all pages/components
  locals.user = await getUserFromCookie(cookies);

  // Guard protected routes
  const isProtected = request.url.includes('/dashboard');
  if (isProtected && !locals.user) {
    return redirect('/login');
  }

  // Continue to the page
  return next();
});

Accessing Locals in Pages

---
// Locals is typed — declare types in env.d.ts
const { user } = Astro.locals;
---
<p>Welcome, {user.name}!</p>

Type Your Locals

src/env.d.ts
/// <reference types="astro/client" />

declare namespace App {
  interface Locals {
    user: { id: string; name: string } | null;
  }
}
16 · Actions

Actions v4.15+

Actions are type-safe server functions you can call from the client — like tRPC but built into Astro. Great for form submissions, mutations, anything that needs a server round-trip.

Defining Actions

src/actions/index.ts
import { defineAction, z } from 'astro:actions';

export const server = {
  newsletter: defineAction({
    accept: 'form',  // or 'json'
    input: z.object({
      email: z.string().email(),
    }),
    handler: async ({ email }) => {
      await subscribeToNewsletter(email);
      return { success: true };
    },
  }),
};

Calling Actions from Client

// From a React component (island)
import { actions } from 'astro:actions';

const handleSubmit = async (e) => {
  const { data, error } = await actions.newsletter({ email: email });
  if (error) return setError(error.message);
  setSuccess(true);
};
💡 Form Progressive Enhancement
Actions support native HTML forms with zero JavaScript as a fallback. The form still works without JS, and hydrates into a client-side experience when JS is available.
17 · View Transitions

View Transitions

Astro has first-class support for the View Transitions API — smooth, app-like page transitions with minimal code.

Enable Globally

src/layouts/BaseLayout.astro
---
import { ViewTransitions } from 'astro:transitions';
---
<head>
  <ViewTransitions />   <!-- That's all! -->
</head>

Named Transitions

<!-- Page A -->
<img
  src="/hero.jpg"
  transition:name="hero-image"
  transition:animate="slide"
/>

<!-- Page B — same name creates a morph transition -->
<img
  src="/hero.jpg"
  transition:name="hero-image"
/>

Built-in Animations

Directive Effect
transition:animate="fade" Fade in/out (default)
transition:animate="slide" Slide left/right
transition:animate="none" No animation
transition:animate={myAnimation} Custom keyframe animation

Persist Elements Across Pages

<!-- Media player stays alive during navigation -->
<AudioPlayer transition:persist />

<!-- Keep scroll position of sidebar -->
<nav transition:persist="sidebar">...</nav>
18 · Environment Variables

Environment Variables

Naming Convention

Prefix Accessible In Example
PUBLIC_ Server + Client (browser) PUBLIC_API_URL
(no prefix) Server only DATABASE_URL 
.env
DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost/mydb     # server only
PUBLIC_API_URL=https://api.mysite.com      # exposed to browser

Accessing Variables

---
// Server-side (both work in .astro frontmatter)
const dbUrl = import.meta.env.DATABASE_URL;
const apiUrl = import.meta.env.PUBLIC_API_URL;
---

// Client-side JS (only PUBLIC_ is available)
const apiUrl = import.meta.env.PUBLIC_API_URL;

Type-Safe Env (Astro v5) NEW

astro.config.mjs
import { defineConfig, envField } from 'astro/config';

export default defineConfig({
  env: {
    schema: {
      DATABASE_URL: envField.string({ context: 'server', access: 'secret' }),
      PUBLIC_API_URL: envField.string({ context: 'client', access: 'public' }),
    }
  }
});
19 · Deployment

Deployment

Static (No Adapter Needed)

If output: 'static', just run npm run build and deploy the dist/ folder anywhere.

Vercel

Zero-config. Connect repo, auto-detects Astro.

Netlify

Drag-drop dist/ or connect Git.

Cloudflare Pages

Build command: npm run build, output: dist.

GitHub Pages

Use @astrojs/github-pages action.

AWS S3 + CloudFront

Upload dist/ to S3, serve via CloudFront.

Any Static Host

NGINX, Apache, self-hosted — dist/ is pure HTML/CSS/JS.

SSR Adapters

# Install the adapter for your platform:
npx astro add vercel      # Vercel Functions
npx astro add netlify     # Netlify Functions
npx astro add cloudflare  # Cloudflare Workers
npx astro add node        # Node.js (self-hosted)
npx astro add deno        # Deno Deploy

Docker (Node Adapter)

Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build
EXPOSE 4321
CMD ["node", "./dist/server/entry.mjs"]
Astro Handbook · Created by Ziad Elnagar ZiadNagar/astro-handbook
